1.2.c.info:
A spine-leaf fabric is a two-tier data-center topology where every leaf switch connects to every spine switch, and nothing else crosses those layers. Leaves never connect to other leaves. Spines never connect to other spines. Servers connect only to leaves; spines never see servers directly.

Why it exists: traditional three-tier was built for north-south traffic (user-to-server). Modern DC workloads are dominated by east-west traffic — VMs talking to VMs, containers to containers, storage replication, microservices. Three-tier penalizes east-west traffic because it has to traverse multiple unpredictable hops (access → distribution → core → distribution → access). Spine-leaf guarantees every server-to-server flow is exactly two hops: leaf → spine → leaf.

Key properties:

  • Full mesh between layers — if you have 4 spines and 16 leaves, that's 64 links.
  • ECMP everywhere — each leaf has equal-cost paths through every spine. L3 routing (BGP or OSPF in the underlay) load-balances across all of them simultaneously.
  • STP is not used in the fabric — because every link is L3 routed, STP has nothing to block.
  • Predictable latency — 2 hops is 2 hops, regardless of which leaf pair you pick.
  • Linear scaling — more leaves = more server ports, more spines = more bandwidth between leaves.

Typical platforms: Cisco Nexus 9000 series (leaf + spine), Arista 7050/7280, Juniper QFX. Often paired with VXLAN EVPN to provide L2 extension across the routed fabric, which is essential for VM mobility.

hack.memory: Spine-leaf = rib cage. Spine = spine (connects only to ribs). Ribs = leaves (connect only to spine + organs = servers). No rib touches another rib. Any two servers are exactly one spine apart.
hack.practice: Draw a spine-leaf with 2 spines + 4 leaves on paper. Count the links (8). Trace a packet from Server A on Leaf 1 to Server B on Leaf 3 — always 2 hops, always Leaf → Spine → Leaf. Add a third spine and note: no leaves change, but every leaf now has 3 paths to every other leaf.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: if the scenario mentions data center + east-west traffic + predictable latency → spine-leaf.

1.2.c.info:
Leaf switches — also called Top-of-Rack (ToR) switches — sit at the access layer of a spine-leaf fabric. One (or a redundant pair) lives in the top of each server rack and connects downward to the servers in that rack and upward to every spine switch in the fabric.

Leaf responsibilities:

  • Server connectivity — typical downlink speeds are 10G, 25G, 100G per server port, depending on generation.
  • L3 boundary — each leaf is an L3 switch. The default gateway for a server's VLAN lives on the leaf (anycast gateway in VXLAN EVPN).
  • VXLAN VTEP (VXLAN Tunnel Endpoint) — leaves encapsulate Ethernet frames into VXLAN tunnels to reach remote leaves across the L3 fabric.
  • Uplinks to every spine — typically 40G, 100G, or 400G. Number of uplinks = number of spines.
  • Server dual-homing — critical servers often connect to two leaves in the same rack via MLAG / vPC for redundancy.

Strict design rule: leaves do not connect to other leaves. If two leaves need to exchange traffic, it must go through a spine. This sounds wasteful but is what makes latency predictable and scaling linear.

Typical hardware: Cisco Nexus 93180YC-FX3 (48x 25G + 6x 100G), Arista 7050X3, Nexus 9336C-FX2. Port density and uplink speed define the leaf's scale.

hack.memory: Leaf = apartment lobby. Every tenant (server) goes through the lobby. Every lobby connects to every elevator shaft (spine). Lobbies never connect directly to each other — you go up through an elevator and down through another lobby.
hack.practice: On paper, label a 2-spine 4-leaf fabric. Count uplinks per leaf = 2 (one to each spine). Count downlinks = however many server ports. Note that leaf-to-leaf links are ILLEGAL in a pure spine-leaf design.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 'What device do servers connect to in a spine-leaf fabric?' = leaf (or ToR).

1.2.c.info:
Spine switches form the backbone of a spine-leaf fabric. They connect only to leaves and never to servers or other spines. Their entire job is high-speed, hardware-forwarded transit between leaves.

Spine responsibilities:

  • Aggregate leaf uplinks — every leaf has one link to every spine.
  • L3 forwarding only — spine runs the underlay routing protocol (typically eBGP or OSPF) and forwards VXLAN-encapsulated packets from one leaf's VTEP to another.
  • ECMP fanout — since each leaf has equal-cost paths through every spine, traffic load-balances across all spines.
  • No policy — same principle as the core in three-tier. ACLs, NAT, and stateful features don't belong on the spine.
  • Port density — a typical spine might have 32-64 high-speed ports (100G or 400G).

Scaling the fabric: add more spines to increase bandwidth between leaves. With 2 spines, each leaf has 2x uplink bandwidth. With 4 spines, 4x. The only limit is how many uplink ports a leaf has.

Strict design rule: spines never connect to other spines, and never connect to servers. Violating this creates traffic patterns that break the predictable 2-hop guarantee.

Typical hardware: Cisco Nexus 9336C-FX2, 9364D-GX2A, Arista 7280R3, 7800R3. Built for non-blocking, line-rate L3 forwarding at high port speeds.

hack.memory: Spine = elevator shafts in a building. Each floor (leaf) has an elevator to every shaft. Shafts don't connect to each other (no tunnel between elevators). Shafts don't go to tenants (no servers on the spine).
hack.practice: Draw a fabric with 2 spines. Calculate aggregate bandwidth between any leaf pair = 2 × uplink speed. Add a third spine. Note: 3 × uplink speed available. Scaling is linear in spine count.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: 'Spine switches connect to ___' = leaves only.

1.2.c.info:
East-west traffic is traffic that flows laterally — server-to-server, VM-to-VM, container-to-container, microservice-to-microservice — within the data center. The name comes from network diagrams where client-to-server (external) traffic is drawn north-south (top-down) and intra-DC traffic flows east-west (side-to-side).

Why east-west dominates modern data centers:

  • Virtualization — VMs communicate heavily with each other: app server → database server → cache server.
  • Microservices — a single user request can trigger dozens of internal service-to-service calls.
  • Distributed storage — replication between storage nodes, erasure coding, and resync can dwarf north-south traffic.
  • AI/ML workloads — GPU-to-GPU traffic for training jobs (all-reduce operations) is extreme east-west with very tight latency requirements.
  • VM mobility — live migration moves GB-scale VM state between hosts.

Why three-tier fails at east-west: two servers on different access switches in different distribution blocks might traverse 6+ hops with highly variable latency. STP blocks redundant links, wasting bandwidth.

Why spine-leaf excels at east-west: any leaf-to-leaf path is exactly 2 hops (leaf → spine → leaf), ECMP uses all spine links in parallel, and latency is consistent regardless of which leaves the two servers happen to live on.

Industry rule of thumb: in a modern DC, east-west traffic is 70-80%+ of total traffic. That single fact is why spine-leaf replaced three-tier in the data center.

hack.memory: East-west = gossip between coworkers on the same floor. North-south = email from the CEO upstairs. Modern office gossips way more than the CEO emails, which is why you optimize for gossip (east-west) in the DC.
hack.practice: Sketch a 3-tier web app: user → load balancer → app server → database → cache. Count the east-west hops vs north-south hops. Compare to a monolith (one box). East-west has exploded.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: know that spine-leaf is optimized for east-west and that east-west dominates modern DC traffic.

1.2.c.info:
North-south traffic is traffic that flows vertically on a network diagram: into and out of the data center (client → server, user → app), or up and down the campus hierarchy (user → WAN → internet).

Classic north-south patterns:

  • User at home → internet → web server in the data center.
  • Branch office user → WAN → HQ data center.
  • Campus user → distribution → core → WAN edge router → internet.

Why three-tier was designed for it: the hierarchical model was invented when almost all traffic was north-south — clients on desktops pulling data from centralized servers. Traffic entered at the access layer, flowed up through distribution to core, and out to the WAN. Every flow followed a predictable vertical path, which the hierarchy handled cleanly.

What changed: virtualization, microservices, big-data pipelines, and distributed storage caused traffic to flip. East-west now dominates, and north-south is the minority in most modern DCs. But north-south is still dominant in the campus (users at their desks accessing apps in the DC or cloud).

Design implication: three-tier remains the right campus architecture precisely because campus traffic is still mostly north-south. Spine-leaf is the right DC architecture because DC traffic is mostly east-west. The two topologies coexist: campus users hit a three-tier, which hands off to a WAN edge, which eventually reaches a DC built on spine-leaf.

hack.memory: North-south = your commute to work (vertical, up and out). East-west = conversations at your desk with coworkers (lateral). Campus = commuting-heavy. DC = conversation-heavy.
hack.practice: Pick a single website visit: browser → ISP → server. Mark each hop north-south or east-west. Then the server behind the scenes: app → DB → cache. Mark those. Observe the ratio flip.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: N-S dominates campus, E-W dominates DC. Match the architecture to the traffic.

1.2.c.info:
ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) is a Layer 3 feature that lets a router load-balance traffic across multiple paths of equal cost to the same destination. In spine-leaf, ECMP is the reason the fabric works — every leaf has N equal-cost paths to every other leaf (one per spine) and uses all of them simultaneously.

How it works:

  • A routing protocol (OSPF, EIGRP, BGP) installs multiple next-hops for the same destination prefix because their costs are identical.
  • For each packet (or each flow), the router picks one of the next-hops using a hash of packet fields — typically 5-tuple: source IP, destination IP, protocol, source port, destination port.
  • Same flow = same hash = same path, so packets in the same flow stay in order.
  • Different flows spread across all paths, achieving load balance without reordering.

Why ECMP replaces STP in spine-leaf: STP's job was to prevent L2 loops by blocking redundant links. In a spine-leaf fabric, every link is L3 routed — loops are prevented by TTL decrement, not by blocking. Every link can forward simultaneously. In a 4-spine fabric, all 4 uplinks per leaf are active, giving 4x the bandwidth of a single-path design.

Configuration: ECMP is usually automatic. OSPF installs up to 4 equal-cost paths by default (tunable with maximum-paths N). BGP requires the maximum-paths knob to enable multipath. The exam-relevant fact: ECMP load-balances across same-cost paths, and it's the mechanism that makes spine-leaf faster than three-tier + STP.

nodes: Pick one of N equal-cost spines

hack.memory: ECMP = multiple checkout lanes at the grocery store, all equally fast. The manager (hash) assigns customers (flows) to lanes. Each customer stays in their lane (no reordering). All lanes are busy. STP was the old rule of 'only one lane open, close the rest.'
hack.practice: OSPF lab: build a router with 3 equal-cost paths to a destination. Run 'show ip route' — you'll see the destination with 3 next-hops. Ping with varied source ports and capture: different flows take different paths, same flow stays on one path.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 25-27 (OSPF). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam: know ECMP = multiple equal-cost paths, and it's what lets spine-leaf use all links simultaneously (unlike STP).

1.2.c.info:
The signature guarantee of spine-leaf: any server-to-server flow between two different leaves traverses exactly 2 hops — leaf → spine → leaf. Not 'usually', not 'on average'. Always.

Why it matters: modern workloads like distributed databases, AI training, and real-time microservices are extremely sensitive to latency jitter (variance). If one flow takes 5 microseconds and another takes 50, the slowest flow becomes the bottleneck for the entire workload. Predictable latency eliminates this tail-latency problem.

Contrast with three-tier:

  • Server A on Access-1 to Server B on Access-2 in the same distribution block: 4 hops (Access → Dist → Dist → Access).
  • Server A to Server B in a different distribution block: 6 hops (Access → Dist → Core → Core → Dist → Access).
  • Same-rack: 2 hops, different-rack: 4 hops, different-block: 6 hops. Latency depends on physical placement.

Spine-leaf comparison:

  • Server A and Server B on the same leaf: 1 hop (via the leaf). Rare but possible.
  • Server A and Server B on different leaves: exactly 2 hops (leaf → spine → leaf). Physical placement doesn't matter.

Corollary — bandwidth is also predictable. ECMP uses all spine paths, so the available bandwidth between any two leaves is N × link-speed regardless of which leaves you pick. No hotspots, no slow corners of the fabric.

hack.memory: Spine-leaf = subway system where every station (leaf) has a direct train to every hub (spine). From any station, one train to a hub, one train back down. Always 2 trains, always predictable.
hack.practice: Draw 5 leaves + 3 spines. Pick any two leaves. Trace the path — always 2 hops. Pick another pair. Same. Now compare to three-tier: pick pairs at increasing distance and watch hops grow.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: if a question mentions 'predictable latency' or 'deterministic performance' in a DC context, the answer is spine-leaf.

1.2.c.info:
VXLAN (Virtual Extensible LAN) is the overlay protocol almost always paired with a spine-leaf fabric. It encapsulates Ethernet frames inside UDP packets so Layer 2 segments can extend across a Layer 3 fabric.

The problem VXLAN solves: spine-leaf is pure L3 — every link is routed, no STP, no native L2 extension. But applications and VMs often expect L2 adjacency (same subnet, same broadcast domain), especially for VM mobility. Without an overlay, you would have to give every leaf L2 membership in every VLAN, which doesn't scale and defeats the L3 design.

How VXLAN works:

  • Each leaf acts as a VTEP (VXLAN Tunnel Endpoint).
  • A VM sends an Ethernet frame destined for another VM.
  • The ingress VTEP (local leaf) wraps the frame with a VXLAN header (24-bit VNI — VXLAN Network Identifier) and a UDP/IP header.
  • The encapsulated packet is routed across the L3 fabric via ECMP across spines.
  • The egress VTEP (remote leaf) strips the encapsulation and delivers the original frame to the destination VM.

Key numbers: VXLAN VNI is 24 bits → 16 million segments (vs 4094 VLANs with 12-bit VLAN IDs). UDP port 4789. Adds 50 bytes of overhead (so MTU on fabric links should be 1600+ or jumbo).

Control plane: modern VXLAN uses EVPN (Ethernet VPN) via BGP to distribute MAC/IP reachability between VTEPs. This is called VXLAN EVPN and is the standard deployment model. Cisco's ACI is a VXLAN EVPN implementation.

layers: Ingress VTEP adds VXLAN + UDP + IP

hack.memory: VXLAN = shipping a letter (Ethernet frame) inside a shipping box (UDP/IP). The L3 fabric sees only the box. When the box arrives at the remote leaf, it opens the box and hands the letter to the VM. 24-bit VNI = 16 million possible boxes.
hack.practice: Research a simple VXLAN diagram. Identify the inner Ethernet frame, the VXLAN header (VNI), the outer UDP (port 4789), the outer IP (VTEP-to-VTEP). You won't configure VXLAN on CCNA, but know the encapsulation and the acronym VTEP.
hack.meta: CCNA exposure is conceptual. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11 touches it. Exam: know VXLAN is the overlay used with spine-leaf, VNI is 24-bit, runs over UDP 4789, and EVPN is the control plane.

1.2.d.info:
A WAN (Wide Area Network) connects networks across geographically separated sites — branch offices, data centers, headquarters, remote workers, cloud providers — over distances that a LAN cannot span. WAN links traverse infrastructure owned by service providers (telcos, ISPs), which introduces cost, latency, and SLAs that LANs don't have.

LAN vs WAN — the defining differences:

  • Distance — LAN: meters to a few km. WAN: km to continents.
  • Ownership — LAN: you own all the cable and gear. WAN: the provider owns the middle.
  • Speed — LAN: 1G-100G+ within a building. WAN: 1 Mbps–10G+ per link, cost per Mbps is way higher.
  • Latency — LAN: sub-millisecond. WAN: 5-300 ms depending on distance.
  • Cost model — LAN: one-time hardware. WAN: recurring monthly fees.
  • Reliability — LAN: extremely reliable. WAN: provider outages happen; you design around them with backup links.

Core WAN pieces:

  • CPE (Customer Premises Equipment) — the router you own at each site.
  • Demarc (demarcation point) — where the provider's responsibility ends and yours begins. Usually a wall-mounted fiber termination or a cable modem.
  • Provider cloud — the infrastructure in the middle you don't control.

Why it's on the exam: every enterprise network has a WAN. You must know the technology options (MPLS, Metro Ethernet, Broadband, Leased Line, SD-WAN), their tradeoffs, and when to pick each.

hack.memory: WAN = highway system between cities. LAN = streets inside one city. You own the streets; the state owns the highways. You pay tolls (monthly fees). You obey their rules (SLAs).
hack.practice: Draw 3 sites (HQ, 2 branches). Label each CPE, the demarcs, and the provider cloud. Compare to drawing a LAN — WAN has way more 'fuzzy cloud' in the middle you don't control.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53 (WAN Architectures). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know LAN vs WAN distinctions and the terms CPE / demarc.

1.2.d.info:
MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching) is a provider-managed, private WAN service that uses short labels instead of IP routing to forward packets across the provider's backbone. To the customer, it looks like a private network where every site can reach every other site with guaranteed performance.

How MPLS forwards:

  • At the provider edge (PE router), each incoming packet gets one or more labels pushed onto its header.
  • Inside the provider core (P routers), forwarding is done by label lookup instead of IP lookup — faster, simpler, and independent of customer IP routes.
  • At the egress PE, the label is popped and the original packet is delivered to the destination site.

Common MPLS service types on CCNA:

  • MPLS L3 VPN — the provider runs a separate routing instance (VRF) per customer and routes between your sites. You peer with the provider via BGP or static routes. Each site looks like a neighbor.
  • MPLS L2 VPN / VPLS — the provider gives you an Ethernet-like L2 service between sites. Your routers see each other as L2 neighbors, even though the provider is actually using MPLS underneath.

Strengths:

  • Guaranteed SLAs — bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet loss all contractually guaranteed.
  • QoS support — provider honors QoS markings end-to-end.
  • Any-to-any connectivity — no hub-and-spoke limitation.
  • Mature and reliable — 20+ years of enterprise deployment.

Weaknesses:

  • Expensive — dollars per Mbps is much higher than broadband.
  • Slow to provision — adding a new site can take weeks.
  • Being displaced — SD-WAN is taking enterprise share because it runs over cheap internet.

hack.memory: MPLS = private toll road. You pay for guaranteed lane width (bandwidth), speed limit (latency), and you can haul anything (QoS respected). Provider slaps a colored sticker (label) on your truck at the entrance and routes by sticker color across the backbone, not by the address on the package.
hack.practice: Draw a customer with HQ + 3 branches connected via MPLS. Each site has a CE router connected to a provider PE. Inside the cloud, packets are label-switched. Compare to internet VPN — MPLS has SLAs, internet doesn't.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam favorite: 'Which WAN provides guaranteed SLAs?' = MPLS. Know L3VPN vs L2VPN at a high level.

1.2.d.info:
Metro Ethernet (Metro-E) is an Ethernet-based WAN service provided within a metropolitan area (a city or region). The provider gives you Ethernet hand-offs at each site (copper or fiber, typical speeds 10 Mbps to 10 Gbps), and under the hood runs whatever technology they like (often MPLS) to deliver an Ethernet experience between your sites.

Why customers love it: you connect at the demarc with a standard Ethernet interface. Your router doesn't need special WAN hardware or protocols — it treats the Metro-E circuit as just another Ethernet port, sometimes with a trunk for multiple services.

MEF (Metro Ethernet Forum) service types:

  • E-Line (Ethernet Line) — point-to-point. One site to one site. Used for data-center to data-center or HQ-to-single-branch links.
  • E-LAN (Ethernet LAN) — multipoint-to-multipoint. Any site can talk to any site as if on the same LAN. Good for a campus spread across the city.
  • E-Tree (Ethernet Tree) — hub-and-spoke. A root site talks to all leaf sites; leaves cannot talk to each other directly. Good for cable/ISP aggregation or security-sensitive branch → HQ designs.

Strengths: cheap per Mbps compared to MPLS within a metro, scalable bandwidth upgrades (just change the service tier), familiar Ethernet interface.

Weaknesses: geographically limited (within a metro), SLAs vary by provider, often no QoS guarantees.

hack.memory: Metro-E = city bus system. Ethernet everywhere. E-Line = express route (2 stops). E-LAN = downtown loop (every stop). E-Tree = park-and-ride (all roads lead to the central hub).
hack.practice: Sketch 4 sites in the same city connected by an E-LAN service. Each site router plugs into a metro-E handoff. Logically, they appear on the same L2 segment. Contrast with E-Line (only 2 sites) and E-Tree (everyone to HQ).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know the three MEF types by name and function.

1.2.d.info:
Broadband is the bucket term for consumer/SMB internet access technologies: DSL, Cable, and Fiber-to-the-Home/Premise. Cheap, widely available, typically no SLA — but often the only practical option for small branches and home offices, and increasingly used as an SD-WAN underlay.

DSL (Digital Subscriber Line) — runs over copper telephone lines. Variants: ADSL (asymmetric — faster download, slower upload), VDSL (faster but distance-limited). Speeds: a few Mbps to ~100 Mbps. Distance from the provider's DSLAM matters — performance degrades with distance.

Cable — runs over coaxial cable infrastructure originally built for TV. Uses DOCSIS (currently DOCSIS 3.1, with 4.0 rolling out). Speeds: 100 Mbps to ~10 Gbps symmetrical at the top end, though most plans are asymmetrical. Shared medium in the neighborhood — performance can drop at peak hours.

Fiber (FTTH / FTTP) — fiber directly to the home or building. Symmetrical gigabit or multi-gigabit speeds. Lowest latency, highest reliability of the broadband tier. Rapidly expanding in residential and SMB markets.

Common properties:

  • No SLA (or very weak SLAs) — consumer-grade service.
  • Cheap per Mbps — 1/5 to 1/50 the cost of MPLS.
  • Best-effort — no QoS honored beyond the CPE.
  • Dynamic public IP usually (static IP often an upgrade).

Enterprise use: broadband is the typical underlay for SD-WAN. SD-WAN builds encrypted tunnels over cheap broadband links and intelligently picks paths, often achieving MPLS-class performance at a fraction of the cost.

hack.memory: Broadband = public highway. Free-ish, goes everywhere, gets crowded at rush hour. MPLS = toll road. SD-WAN = a GPS that picks the best highway or toll road per trip.
hack.practice: Compare your home internet (likely cable or fiber) to an enterprise MPLS link — same Mbps, but 10-50x cheaper and no SLA. This is why SD-WAN + broadband is replacing MPLS for branches.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know DSL / Cable / Fiber as broadband technologies. DOCSIS for cable.

1.2.d.info:
Leased lines are dedicated, point-to-point circuits with guaranteed bandwidth, rented from a provider. Legacy technology, still tested on the CCNA but largely replaced in production by Metro Ethernet and MPLS.

North American hierarchy:

  • T1 — 1.544 Mbps. 24 DS0 channels (each 64 kbps). Classic enterprise leased line of the 1990s-2000s.
  • T3 — 44.736 Mbps. 28 T1s. Used for larger sites or aggregation.

European / international hierarchy:

  • E1 — 2.048 Mbps. 32 DS0 channels.
  • E3 — 34.368 Mbps. 16 E1s.

Properties:

  • Dedicated bandwidth — the full rate is yours, not shared.
  • Predictable latency and low jitter.
  • Expensive — hundreds to thousands per month for modest bandwidth.
  • Slow to provision — telcos can take weeks to install.
  • Point-to-point only — one circuit = two endpoints. Any-to-any requires a mesh of circuits.

Why they're fading: Metro Ethernet gives you 100 Mbps for less than a T1's 1.544 Mbps in many markets. MPLS gives you any-to-any without the mesh of circuits. Leased lines persist mostly for specialized use cases (government, military, low-bandwidth legacy sites).

Exam-worthy trivia: T1 = 1.544 Mbps and T3 = 44.736 Mbps — memorize these two numbers, they're commonly tested.

hack.memory: Leased line = private road between two buildings. Bulletproof reliability, expensive, one-to-one only. T1 = 1.544 Mbps (say it like a phone number: '1.544'). T3 = 44.736 (harder to memorize — just grind it).
hack.practice: Flashcard: T1 / T3 / E1 / E3 speeds. Also learn that a T1 has 24 DS0 × 64 kbps = 1.536 Mbps payload, plus framing = 1.544 Mbps total. E1 has 32 DS0 × 64 = 2.048 Mbps.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: pure memorization — T1 = 1.544 Mbps, T3 = 44.736 Mbps. Free points if you have them locked in.

1.2.d.info:
SD-WAN (Software-Defined WAN) is the modern approach to enterprise WAN. Instead of one expensive private circuit per branch, SD-WAN builds encrypted overlay tunnels across whatever transport is available — MPLS, broadband internet, 4G/5G LTE, satellite — and uses a centralized controller to steer traffic intelligently based on application requirements and real-time path quality.

Core architecture:

  • Edge devices at each site run SD-WAN software and establish encrypted tunnels (typically IPsec) to other sites.
  • Controller / orchestrator — centralized management plane. Pushes policy, monitors tunnel health.
  • Underlays — any IP transport works: MPLS, internet, LTE, etc. Often a mix (active/active).
  • Overlay — the encrypted fabric that connects all sites logically, independent of underlay.

Key features:

  • Application-aware routing — route Office 365 traffic via the best-performing path in real time; route bulk backup via the cheapest path.
  • Transport independence — swap MPLS for broadband at will; applications don't know the underlay changed.
  • Dynamic path selection — monitor loss/latency/jitter on each path, switch flows automatically when one degrades.
  • Zero-touch provisioning — ship a device to a branch, plug it in, it phones home and auto-configures.
  • Centralized policy — one policy change propagates to all sites.

Cisco products: Cisco SD-WAN (acquired from Viptela) is the flagship. Meraki SD-WAN is the lighter-touch option. Both are CCNA-relevant only conceptually.

Why it's displacing MPLS: same or better application performance + dramatically lower WAN cost + centralized management. The industry is shifting fast.

children.name: Central controller picks best path per app

hack.memory: SD-WAN = smart GPS for your WAN. You have MPLS, cable, and LTE available. SD-WAN tests all three every second and routes Zoom over the one with best latency, Netflix over the cheapest, and backups over whichever is idle. Central controller = the brain; edge devices = the car.
hack.practice: Sketch 3 branches with two uplinks each (MPLS + broadband). Overlay encrypted tunnels in color between all sites. Imagine the controller measuring loss/latency and moving Zoom flows to whichever tunnel is best at that moment.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53 covers SD-WAN conceptually. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know SD-WAN uses encrypted overlay, central controller, app-aware path selection, and runs over any transport — and that it is replacing MPLS.

1.2.d.info:
CPE (Customer Premises Equipment) is any network hardware owned by the customer at their site — most commonly the edge router (or SD-WAN appliance) that connects the site to the WAN. The term also covers cable modems, firewalls, and any other device the customer manages.

Why the term matters: it defines the responsibility boundary. The provider is responsible for everything from their backbone up to the demarc. The customer is responsible for the CPE and everything behind it. Troubleshooting and SLA discussions hinge on this boundary.

Typical CPE examples:

  • Cisco ISR 4000 / 1000 router at a branch.
  • Cisco Meraki MX security appliance (SD-WAN + firewall).
  • ASR 1000 at a larger campus.
  • Firewall appliance (Palo Alto, Fortinet) if placed at the edge.

Managed CPE vs unmanaged CPE:

  • Managed — the provider owns and manages the CPE on your behalf. You pay them a monthly fee and they handle config, upgrades, monitoring.
  • Unmanaged — you own and manage the CPE yourself. Cheaper, more control, more work.

Exam framing: 'Which device is the CPE?' = the customer-owned router at the edge of the WAN. CPE is behind the demarc on the customer side.

hack.memory: CPE = the last thing you own before the provider takes over. Like the meter box on your house — everything inside is yours, everything outside belongs to the utility.
hack.practice: At home, identify your CPE: the ISP's modem/router (often provider-owned — 'managed') or your own router behind it. Note the clear demarc — their cable ends, your network begins.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: vocabulary question — know CPE = customer-owned edge device.

1.2.d.info:
The demarcation point (demarc) is the physical boundary between the service provider's network and the customer's network. It is usually a labeled termination panel, jack, or fiber patch in a building's telecom room.

Why it's critical:

  • Responsibility line — everything on the provider's side is the provider's problem. Everything on the customer's side is the customer's problem.
  • Troubleshooting anchor — when a WAN link is broken, the first question is 'does the signal reach the demarc?' If yes → customer side; if no → provider.
  • SLA boundary — providers measure SLA performance at the demarc, not inside the customer's network.

Physical forms:

  • RJ-45 jack on a wall plate (for Ethernet hand-offs).
  • Fiber SC/LC termination panel (for fiber hand-offs).
  • Smartjack / NIU (Network Interface Unit) for older circuit-based services.
  • DSL splitter/modem handoff at a home or SMB.

CPE vs demarc: demarc is the point. CPE is the equipment on the customer side of that point. The customer's router connects to the demarc via a jumper cable. From the demarc outward is the provider's loop and backbone.

Field phrase: 'smart demarc' or 'managed demarc' means the provider's responsibility extends a bit further into the building (e.g., they own a small NID box mounted on your wall). Regardless, there's always a line that separates 'their problem' from 'your problem.'

hack.memory: Demarc = the property line of your house on a map. Everything on your side is your yard (your job to mow). Everything on the other side is the city's (their sidewalk, their tree). Fight at the property line when deciding whose outage it is.
hack.practice: In your home, find where the ISP's cable/fiber enters the house — that's your demarc. Note which side of it the provider is responsible for.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. Exam: know demarc = provider/customer boundary and that providers measure SLA at the demarc.

1.2.d.info:
This comparison is the single most tested WAN concept on CCNA. Memorize the tradeoffs; you will almost certainly see at least one question.

MPLS — strengths:

  • Provider-managed, private, predictable — guaranteed SLA on bandwidth/latency/jitter/loss.
  • QoS honored end-to-end.
  • Mature, reliable, 20+ years of enterprise use.
  • Any-to-any connectivity without building a mesh.

MPLS — weaknesses:

  • Expensive — dollars per Mbps far above broadband.
  • Slow to provision — weeks to months.
  • Single transport — if the MPLS circuit dies, the site is cut off (unless a backup exists).
  • Provider-dependent — hard to change providers.

SD-WAN — strengths:

  • Uses any transport — MPLS, broadband, LTE — and often multiple simultaneously.
  • Application-aware routing — pick the best path per app in real time.
  • Dramatically cheaper per Mbps — broadband underlays slash cost.
  • Centralized management — policy pushed from one pane of glass.
  • Zero-touch provisioning — branch setup in minutes, not weeks.
  • Active/active links — both underlays carry traffic, no wasted bandwidth.

SD-WAN — weaknesses:

  • Depends on underlay quality — if all your underlays are congested, SD-WAN can't create bandwidth.
  • Newer technology — less operational maturity in some orgs.
  • Controller is a single point of trust (though typically redundant in cloud).

Exam-winning phrasing:

  • 'Guaranteed SLA' → MPLS.
  • 'Lower WAN cost / uses internet links' → SD-WAN.
  • 'Application-aware path selection' → SD-WAN.
  • 'Centralized management' → SD-WAN.
  • 'Strategic direction of modern enterprise WAN' → SD-WAN.

hack.memory: MPLS = private jet (guaranteed arrival, expensive, one route). SD-WAN = Uber + Lyft + subway (apps pick the best ride each trip, way cheaper, uses any road).
hack.practice: Build a 2-column whiteboard: MPLS pros/cons on left, SD-WAN pros/cons on right. Memorize 3 exam trigger phrases per column. Quiz yourself until instant.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 14. 100% guaranteed on the exam. The Cisco exam writers love this comparison.
